message.content:
CONFIGURED-panel review (one of 2; return verdict JSON, write NOTHING). Round 1.
Task: T73 (goals:G5) — "Implement the re-resolution controller: poll plus liveness-loss triggers, change-suppressed repoint" (full text: fetch_item tasks T73). ALSO covers defect D46 (never-publish-empty retention — you filed it).
Worktree: /home/pavel/work/safe/fecalia/.claude/worktrees/agent-ac373e4ef09d0a464 (branch implement/T73, base 8c0411d)
Go repo — gate incl. `go test ./internal/device/... -race` via nix develop.
acceptance: go test ./internal/device/... -race with injected resolver + fake clock: (1) lookup failure every tick leaves the set untouched, keeps retrying; (2) changed active IP → exactly one SetPeerRemote; (3) unchanged IP → none over many ticks; (4) all-paths-down triggers a re-resolve before the next poll tick; (5) TTL below poll interval shortens the next resolve delay; PLUS (D46) empty/NXDOMAIN for a previously-resolved hostname retains last-good.
Worker: pass; commit 0857f95; files internal/device/{resolution.go(new),resolution_test.go(new),failover.go}, docs/design.md. Design: step() edge-triggered + nextPollAt gating via injected clock; coordinates with hubFailover via shared lock + updateResolution; allPathsDown/activeSpecIndex accessors; D46 fix = skip updateResolution on error/empty. Scrutinize: the goroutine lifecycle (stop path, leak-freedom), the liveness-loss edge-trigger's re-arm condition, family-filter/ordering determinism, and whether the controller genuinely runs for a single-hostname peer.
Return { taskId, verdict, criticism: [], questions: [], defects: [], rationale, summary }.

attachment.content:
- baboon: Baboon data model compiler reference. Covers model syntax, multi-version evolution, code generation, wire-format conversions, database migrations, backward compatibility, and version negotiation. TRIGGER when: designing, changing, or discussing any external API, working with .baboon model files, updating data models, adding new fields or types, bumping model versions, running code generation, implementing conversions, writing database migrations, or discussing API versioning strategy.
- constructive-test-taxonomy: Multi-axis classification of tests (Intention × Encapsulation × Isolation) for evaluating test quality and steering test-writing decisions. Replaces fuzzy Unit/Integration/E2E vocabulary with precise tags and a maintenance-cost heuristic. TRIGGER when: writing new tests, reviewing a test suite, deciding what to test or at what level, discussing mocks vs. real dependencies, classifying tests, or arguing about test coverage or test-strategy tradeoffs.
- dual-tests: Pattern for testing code that touches external systems: write one abstract test suite against an interface, then run it against both the production adapter (real DB/API/FS) and a hand-written in-memory dummy. Prefers manual dummies over auto-generated mocks. TRIGGER when: designing code that depends on a database, HTTP API, message queue, filesystem, or other external system; choosing between mocks, fakes, or dummies; adding integration points; setting up test infrastructure; or when the `constructive-test-taxonomy` skill calls for moving Communication-tier tests toward Blackbox-Atomic.
- environment: Bubblewrap "yolo" sandbox escape workflow (exchange scripts for reaching $HOME, system paths, or files outside the sandbox) plus in-sandbox nix/direnv usage. TRIGGER only when $SMIND_SANDBOXED=1 — i.e. running inside the yolo sandbox and you need to reach something outside it. SKIP entirely when $SMIND_SANDBOXED is unset: you are unsandboxed, so write directly and ignore this skill.
- izumi: Best-practice playbook for the izumi Scala ecosystem — distage (DI), distage-framework (roles/RoleAppMain), distage-testkit (test harness with memoization), distage-framework-docker (test containers), logstage (structured logging), and BIO (bifunctor effect typeclasses). Covers idiomatic bindings, Activation/Axis switching, Lifecycle discipline, plugin layout, and how to apply the `dual-tests` pattern inside distage so one test body runs against both a dummy and a real-DB adapter. TRIGGER when: writing or reviewing Scala code that imports `izumi.distage.*`, `izumi.logstage.*`, `izumi.functional.bio.*`, `distage.*`, `logstage.*`; designing a new distage plugin or Role; wiring a testkit `SpecZIO`/`Spec2`/`Spec1`; configuring `ContainerDef`/`DockerSupportModule`; choosing between `StandardAxis.Repo` or `Scene` tags; or implementing the dual-tests pattern in a distage project. The canonical reference project is https://github.com/7mind/distage-example.
- resilient-ws-ui: Guidelines for building applications that maintain reliable WebSocket connections to a backend and surface connection health clearly to the user. Invoke ONLY when the user explicitly references this skill by name (e.g. "/resilient-ws-ui", "use the resilient-ws-ui skill"). Do NOT auto-invoke based on topic match.
- tass: Target/Actual State Separation (TASS) pattern for reliable state management in event-driven control systems. TRIGGER when: implementing device or entity state management, designing control systems, working with target vs actual state separation, or building event-driven systems with commanded/observed state.
- cq:advance: Advance the WHOLE flow one cycle — chain /cq:investigate:advance, /cq:plan:advance, /cq:implement:advance to quiescence, then report DRAINED / BLOCKED-ON-QUESTIONS / MIXED.
- cq:implement-review: Implement-flow adversarial per-task review rubric + stdout-JSON contract, as a portable promptTemplate for non-Claude harnesses (pi/Codex). Symmetric to cq:plan-review. Judges ONE task's implementation, splits findings into autonomously-fixable criticism[], user-only questions[], and out-of-scope defects[], and emits the verdict as JSON on stdout. Writes NOTHING to the ledger — the orchestrator records the terminal review.
- cq:implement:advance: Advance an implement-flow run one full pass — pick DAG-ready tasks, implement each in an isolated worktree, adversarially review, autonomously fix criticism, register user questions, and merge back in dependency order.
- cq:implement:start: Start an implement-flow run — resolve the target milestones (default: all open ones), validate their task DAG, then hand off to the /cq:implement:advance loop.
- cq:investigate: Start an investigate-flow run — defect intake + bootstrap, then run the /cq:investigate:advance pass inline.
- cq:investigate:advance: Advance an investigate-flow run one research round — read defect state, form/extend the hypothesis tree, dispatch read-only explorers, validate every citation against source, adjudicate node status, and on a confirmed root cause file-and-defer the fix to plan-flow.
- cq:plan: Start a plan-flow goal — create the goal (from free text OR one-or-more idea-ids, one goal per idea), then hand off to the planner for the first clarifying questions.
- cq:plan-review: Shared adversarial plan-review rubric — judge an emitted plan for a goal against the fine-grained / sequenced / testable / grounded / complete rubric, classify findings into new_questions / criticism / defects, and emit a single fenced-json verdict on stdout. The CANONICAL rubric source; Claude's plan-reviewer agent and every non-Claude (Codex/Pi) reviewer judge the same way and emit the same JSON.
- cq:plan:advance: Advance plan-flow goals one full round — a given goal, or (no argument) every unlocked goal — running the planner↔reviewer loop until each needs the user or reaches `planned`.
- cq:plan:follow-up: Add new scope to an EXISTING plan-flow goal — append the follow-up request, re-open the goal, and hand to the planner for a fresh clarifying round.
- cq:planners: Session-only planner-set override — parse a natural-language planner instruction (e.g. "use grok and opus only") into canonical harness:model tokens via the cq.toml [aliases] table (from the ledger MCP get_planners/get_config) plus a documented fallback map; echo the resolved active set; confirm the override is SESSION-ONLY and reverts on the next fresh run.
- cq:reviewers: Session-only reviewer-set override — parse a natural-language reviewer instruction (e.g. "use grok and opus only") into canonical harness:model tokens via the cq.toml [aliases] table (from the ledger MCP get_reviewers/get_config) plus a documented fallback map; echo the resolved active set; confirm the override is SESSION-ONLY and reverts on the next fresh run.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- codex:rescue: Delegate investigation, an explicit fix request, or follow-up rescue work to the Codex rescue subagent
- codex:setup: Check whether the local Codex CLI is ready and optionally toggle the stop-time review gate
- codex:codex-cli-runtime: Internal helper contract for calling the codex-companion runtime from Claude Code
- codex:codex-result-handling: Internal guidance for presenting Codex helper output back to the user
- codex:gpt-5-4-prompting: Internal guidance for composing Codex and GPT-5.4 prompts for coding, review, diagnosis, and research tasks inside the Codex Claude Code plugin
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

New controller in internal/device (e.g. resolution.go), mirroring the hubFailover shape: pure constructor over injected collaborators (dnsresolve.Resolver, the mutable endpoint set's update API, the same hubHealth liveness plane, telemetry.Clock, poll interval from [dns]) so it is unit-testable with a fake resolver and fake clock (Q36). One evaluation step: for each hostname spec, Lookup with the configured timeout; on SUCCESS, family-filter/order and call updateResolution (change detection inside); on FAILURE, keep the previous expansion and retry next tick — a lookup failure NEVER tears down a working endpoint set and never hard-fails anything (Q30 tolerant model). Liveness-loss trigger: when every path to the ACTIVE endpoint is DOWN (same allDown sweep the failover controller uses), trigger an immediate out-of-band re-resolve of the active spec rather than waiting for the next tick; coordinate with hubFailover purely through the shared lock and the update API (Q34) so the two controllers cannot fight over the bond (each repoint is a single guarded SetPeerRemote). Resolution runs entirely off the send hot path (its own goroutine; results applied under the endpoint-set lock only). content.content:
1	package device
2	
3	import (
4		"context"
5		"net/netip"
6		"sync"
7		"time"
8	
9		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/config"
10		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/dnsresolve"
11		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/log"
12		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/telemetry"
13	)
14	
15	// nameTarget is one hostname endpoint spec the re-resolution controller tracks: the index of
16	// its owning failoverSpec (which equals the entry's position in config.Peer.EndpointSpecs, since
17	// the failover specs are built 1:1 in TOML order) together with the host/port to resolve. Only
18	// hostname specs (EndpointSpec.IsName) become nameTargets; literal specs never re-resolve.
19	type nameTarget struct {
20		specIdx int
21		host    string
22		port    uint16
23	}
24	
25	// resolution is the edge-side re-resolution controller (Q31/T73). It re-resolves each opted-in
26	// hostname peer-endpoint spec on a fixed poll cadence AND out-of-band the instant every path to
27	// the ACTIVE concentrator goes DOWN, then hands the fresh record set to hubFailover's update API
28	// so the bond is repointed only on an ACTUAL change (D32 suppression lives inside updateResolution).
29	//
30	// It mirrors the hubFailover shape: a pure constructor over injected collaborators (a
31	// dnsresolve.Resolver, the hubFailover whose update API / active-spec identity / shared liveness
32	// plane it drives, a telemetry.Clock, and the [dns] poll interval + per-lookup timeout), so its
33	// decision logic is unit-testable against a fake resolver, a fake clock, and a hubFailover built
34	// over fake health/remote. Resolution runs entirely OFF the send hot path — its own goroutine
35	// performs the network lookups; results are applied only through updateResolution under the
36	// endpoint-set lock (Q34: the two controllers coordinate purely through that shared lock and the
37	// update API, never a second, competing repoint path).
38	//
39	// RETENTION INVARIANT (D46): a lookup that FAILS (error/timeout/NXDOMAIN) or yields an EMPTY
40	// address set NEVER publishes — the spec keeps its last-good expansion and the controller retries
41	// next tick. A working endpoint set is therefore never torn down by a transient resolver fault,
42	// and hubFailover never sees an empty active spec that its total<2 guard could strand the bond on.
43	type resolution struct {
44		resolver dnsresolve.Resolver
45		// fo is the hub-failover controller this resolver drives: the sole mutation point
46		// (updateResolution), the active-spec identity, and the shared liveness plane all live there.
47		fo *hubFailover
48		// targets is the ordered set of hostname specs to re-resolve; empty for an all-literal peer,
49		// in which case the loop is a no-op.
50		targets []nameTarget
51	
52		clock        telemetry.Clock
53		pollInterval time.Duration
54		timeout      time.Duration
55		log          log.Logger
56	
57		mu sync.Mutex
58		// nextPollAt is when the next SCHEDULED full poll of every hostname spec is due (clock time).
59		// Initialized to construction time so the first step polls immediately (prompt boot adoption
60		// for a hostname-only peer). Advanced by the TTL-clamped poll delay after each poll, and reset
61		// after an out-of-band liveness-loss re-resolve so the two do not double-resolve back to back.
62		// Guarded by mu.
63		nextPollAt time.Time
64		// lastAllDown is the previous liveness-loss reading, so the out-of-band re-resolve is
65		// EDGE-triggered (fires once when hub loss is first observed, not every tick while it
66		// persists — the poll loop covers a sustained outage without hammering the resolver).
67		// Guarded by mu.
68		lastAllDown bool
69	}
70	
71	// newResolution builds the controller over its injected collaborators. It is a pure constructor
72	// (no goroutine, no engine dependency) so a test drives step/pollAll directly against a fake
73	// resolver and a fake clock. nextPollAt is armed at construction time so the first step polls
74	// immediately.
75	func newResolution(resolver dnsresolve.Resolver, fo *hubFailover, targets []nameTarget, clock telemetry.Clock, pollInterval, timeout time.Duration, lg log.Logger) *resolution {
76		return &resolution{
77			resolver:     resolver,
78			fo:           fo,
79			targets:      targets,
80			clock:        clock,
81			pollInterval: pollInterval,
82			timeout:      timeout,
83			log:          lg,
84			nextPollAt:   clock.Now(),
85		}
86	}
87	
88	// nameTargetsFromSpecs extracts the hostname specs (EndpointSpec.IsName) from an ordered spec
89	// list as nameTargets, tagging each with its index in that list — which equals its failoverSpec
90	// index, since both are built 1:1 in TOML order. Literal specs contribute nothing.
91	func nameTargetsFromSpecs(specs []config.EndpointSpec) []nameTarget {
92		var ts []nameTarget
93		for i, s := range specs {
94			if s.IsName {
95				ts = append(ts, nameTarget{specIdx: i, host: s.Host, port: s.Port})
96			}
97		}
98		return ts
99	}
100	
101	// step is one evaluation at the probe cadence: it first services a liveness-loss edge (an
102	// out-of-band re-resolve of the active spec the instant every path goes DOWN), then, if the
103	// scheduled poll is due, re-resolves every hostname spec and re-arms the next poll at the
104	// TTL-clamped cadence. It is cheap in the steady state (a liveness read plus a clock compare)
105	// and performs its network lookups off any lock, so a periodic loop may call it at probe cadence.
106	func (r *resolution) step() {
107		r.checkLivenessLoss()
108	
109		r.mu.Lock()
110		due := !r.clock.Now().Before(r.nextPollAt)
111		r.mu.Unlock()
112		if !due {
113			return
114		}
115		delay := r.pollAll()
116		r.mu.Lock()
117		r.nextPollAt = r.clock.Now().Add(delay)
118		r.mu.Unlock()
119	}
120	
121	// pollAll re-resolves every tracked hostname spec once and returns the delay until the next
122	// scheduled poll: the configured poll interval, CLAMPED to the smallest positive TTL reported by
123	// any spec whose transport exposes one (DoH/DoT ttlOk) — the Q31 TTL nice-to-have — so a record
124	// about to expire is re-checked no later than its TTL. A lookup failure or empty result leaves
125	// that spec's expansion untouched (RETENTION INVARIANT) and does not shorten the delay.
126	func (r *resolution) pollAll() time.Duration {
127		delay := r.pollInterval
128		for _, t := range r.targets {
129			minTTL, ttlOk := r.resolveTarget(t)
130			if ttlOk && minTTL > 0 && minTTL < delay {
131				delay = minTTL
132			}
133		}
134		return delay
135	}
136	
137	// resolveTarget performs one context-bounded lookup for a single hostname spec and, on a
138	// non-empty SUCCESS, hands the family-ordered record set to hubFailover.updateResolution (which
139	// suppresses a no-op change and repoints only on an actual active-IP change). It returns the
140	// lookup's minimum TTL and whether that TTL is meaningful, for the caller's clamp.
141	//
142	// RETENTION INVARIANT (D46): on ANY failure (error/timeout/NXDOMAIN) OR an empty ordered set
143	// (NODATA / a CNAME-only chain / a family the local paths cannot use), it publishes NOTHING —
144	// the spec keeps its previous expansion — and reports (0, false). A previously-resolved hostname
145	// is therefore never collapsed to empty, which is exactly the condition that could strand the
146	// bond under hubFailover's total<2 guard.
147	func (r *resolution) resolveTarget(t nameTarget) (time.Duration, bool) {
148		ctx, cancel := r.lookupContext()
149		defer cancel()
150	
151		addrs, minTTL, ttlOk, err := r.resolver.Lookup(ctx, t.host)
152		if err != nil {
153			r.log.Debug("dns re-resolution: lookup failed; retaining last-good records",
154				"host", t.host, "spec_index", t.specIdx, "error", err.Error())
155			return 0, false
156		}
157		eps := orderAddrPorts(addrs, t.port)
158		if len(eps) == 0 {
159			r.log.Debug("dns re-resolution: empty record set; retaining last-good records",
160				"host", t.host, "spec_index", t.specIdx)
161			return 0, false
162		}
163		r.fo.updateResolution(t.specIdx, eps)
164		return minTTL, ttlOk
165	}
166	
167	// checkLivenessLoss fires the out-of-band re-resolve of the ACTIVE spec on the DOWN edge — the
168	// tick at which every path to the active concentrator first reads DOWN. It coordinates with
169	// hubFailover purely through the shared liveness read (allPathsDown) and active-spec identity
170	// (activeSpecIndex): a hub-loss advance and a liveness-loss re-resolve therefore derive from one
171	// detector. The trigger is edge-triggered so a sustained outage re-resolves once here (the poll
172	// loop covers the rest) rather than on every tick.
173	func (r *resolution) checkLivenessLoss() {
174		down := r.fo.allPathsDown()
175	
176		r.mu.Lock()
177		edge := down && !r.lastAllDown
178		r.lastAllDown = down
179		r.mu.Unlock()
180		if !edge {
181			return
182		}
183	
184		active := r.fo.activeSpecIndex()
185		t, ok := r.targetFor(active)
186		if !ok {
187			// The active spec is a literal (or there is no active entry yet): nothing to re-resolve.
188			return
189		}
190		r.log.Warn("dns re-resolution: all paths to active concentrator down; out-of-band re-resolve",
191			"host", t.host, "spec_index", t.specIdx)
192		r.resolveTarget(t)
193	
194		// An out-of-band re-resolve subsumes the scheduled poll for this instant; push the next poll
195		// out a full interval so the two do not resolve back to back.
196		r.mu.Lock()
197		r.nextPollAt = r.clock.Now().Add(r.pollInterval)
198		r.mu.Unlock()
199	}
200	
201	// targetFor returns the nameTarget owning specIdx, or ok=false when no tracked hostname spec has
202	// that index (a literal active spec, or specIdx == -1 for no active entry).
203	func (r *resolution) targetFor(specIdx int) (nameTarget, bool) {
204		if specIdx < 0 {
205			return nameTarget{}, false
206		}
207		for _, t := range r.targets {
208			if t.specIdx == specIdx {
209				return t, true
210			}
211		}
212		return nameTarget{}, false
213	}
214	
215	// lookupContext returns a context bounded by the configured per-lookup timeout (a non-positive
216	// timeout yields an unbounded context — the caller's config validation keeps it positive).
217	func (r *resolution) lookupContext() (context.Context, context.CancelFunc) {
218		if r.timeout <= 0 {
219			return context.WithCancel(context.Background())
220		}
221		return context.WithTimeout(context.Background(), r.timeout)
222	}
223	
224	// orderAddrPorts turns a resolver's mixed A/AAAA record set into the ordered, deduplicated
225	// []netip.AddrPort a failoverSpec expansion requires: IPv4 records first (in resolver order),
226	// then IPv6, each paired with the spec's port. The order is DETERMINISTIC so an unchanged DNS
227	// answer produces a byte-identical expansion tick after tick — the precondition for
228	// updateResolution's active-AddrPort survival check to suppress a no-op repoint. Invalid or
229	// duplicate addrs are dropped.
230	func orderAddrPorts(addrs []netip.Addr, port uint16) []netip.AddrPort {
231		seen := make(map[netip.Addr]struct{}, len(addrs))
232		var v4, v6 []netip.AddrPort
233		for _, a := range addrs {
234			if !a.IsValid() {
235				continue
236			}
237			canon := a.Unmap()
238			if _, dup := seen[canon]; dup {
239				continue
240			}
241			seen[canon] = struct{}{}
242			ap := netip.AddrPortFrom(canon, port)
243			if canon.Is4() {
244				v4 = append(v4, ap)
245			} else {
246				v6 = append(v6, ap)
247			}
248		}
249		return append(v4, v6...)
250	}
251	
252	// startResolutionLoop launches the re-resolution goroutine: it calls step every interval until
253	// the returned stopper is invoked (idempotent). It mirrors startHubFailoverLoop — a wall-clock
254	// ticker at the probe cadence driving a decision method whose every timing choice runs through
255	// the injected Clock, so a test drives step directly against a fake clock and never starts this
256	// goroutine. It is a no-op (no-op stopper) for a controller with no hostname specs to track or a
257	// non-positive interval.
258	func (r *resolution) startResolutionLoop(interval time.Duration) (stop func()) {
259		if len(r.targets) == 0 || interval <= 0 {
260			return func() {}
261		}
262		done := make(chan struct{})
263		go func() {
264			ticker := time.NewTicker(interval)
265			defer ticker.Stop()
266			for {
267				select {
268				case <-done:
269					return
270				case <-ticker.C:
271					r.step()
272				}
273			}
274		}()
275		var once sync.Once
276		return func() { once.Do(func() { close(done) }) }
277	}
278

content.content:
diff --git a/docs/design.md b/docs/design.md
index dc163f2..d42f702 100644
--- a/docs/design.md
+++ b/docs/design.md
@@ -229,10 +229,12 @@ Two *different* failovers exist and must not be conflated:
   uplinks cannot help; the edge must move to a *standby concentrator*.
 
 An edge peer carries an **ordered** concentrator endpoint list
-(`config.Peer.Endpoints`, Q18/T54): index 0 is the active/primary hub, the rest
-are ordered standbys. Endpoints are **IP:port only — no DNS resolution** (the T54
-constraint). All hubs in the set share the peer's **single WireGuard static key**,
-so the same peer identity re-handshakes against whichever hub is active.
+(`config.Peer.EndpointSpecs`, Q18/T54/Q35): index 0 is the active/primary hub, the
+rest are ordered standbys. Each entry is either an **IP:port literal** or, behind
+the peer's explicit `dns = true` opt-in (Q29), a **hostname:port** whose record set
+is resolved at runtime (see *Re-resolution* below). All hubs in the set share the
+peer's **single WireGuard static key**, so the same peer identity re-handshakes
+against whichever hub is active.
 
 The controller (`hubFailover`) runs a device-lifecycle monitor loop (started after
 `dev.Up`, stopped before `dev.Close`, alongside the probe/reconcile loops):
@@ -290,6 +292,38 @@ this guard are validated by the real-network netns e2e (`TestHubFailoverStandbyS
 + `TestHubFailoverSingleEndpointGuard`, T62) and, over the real internet, by the
 realhosts mid-transfer WAN-kill tier (`TestRealMidTransferWANKill`, T63).
 
+**Re-resolution** (`resolution`, T73). A hostname endpoint spec has no fixed
+address; its expansion is a **mutable, spec-keyed record set** the failover set
+carries (`failoverSpec.addrs`, updated in place by `hubFailover.updateResolution`
+under the endpoint-set lock — the sole mutation point). The re-resolution
+controller keeps those record sets fresh. It mirrors the `hubFailover` shape (a pure
+constructor over an injected `dnsresolve.Resolver`, the failover controller it
+drives, a `telemetry.Clock`, and the `[dns]` poll interval + per-lookup timeout) and
+runs its own device-lifecycle loop **off the send hot path** — all lookups happen on
+its goroutine; results are applied only through `updateResolution`. Each evaluation:
+
+- **Poll** every hostname spec on the fixed `[dns]` cadence; on a **successful,
+  non-empty** lookup the addrs are family-ordered (IPv4 first, then IPv6, deduped —
+  a deterministic order so an unchanged answer yields a byte-identical expansion) and
+  handed to `updateResolution`, which **repoints only on an actual active-IP change**
+  (D32 no-op suppression). When the transport exposes a TTL (DoH/DoT), the next poll
+  is clamped to `min(pollInterval, minTTL)`.
+- **Liveness-loss trigger**: the instant every path to the **active** endpoint reads
+  `StateDown` — the *same* `allDown` sweep the failover loop advances on (Q34: the
+  two controllers coordinate purely through the shared lock and the update API) — the
+  active spec is re-resolved **out of band**, edge-triggered, without waiting for the
+  next poll tick.
+- **Retention invariant (D46)**: a lookup that **fails** (error/timeout/NXDOMAIN) or
+  yields an **empty** set **never publishes** — the spec keeps its last-good
+  expansion and the controller retries next tick. A transient resolver fault
+  therefore never tears down a working endpoint set, and `hubFailover` never sees a
+  previously-resolved active spec collapse to empty (the condition its `total < 2`
+  guard could otherwise strand the bond on).
+
+This controller runs **even for a single-hostname peer** (to track a changing DDNS
+address), independent of hub-failover's `>= 2` guard; the first successful poll of a
+hostname-only peer is what boot-adopts its active endpoint.
+
 ### Per-path telemetry — `internal/telemetry`
 
 Measures per-path quality (RTT, loss, jitter) by exchanging authenticated PROBE

content.text:
All evidence gathered. The gate is green (`go test ./internal/device/... -race`, plus build/vet and adjacent `config`/`dnsresolve` packages), the diff is surgical, docs are synced, and four of the five scrutiny items hold up: the goroutine lifecycle mirrors `startHubFailoverLoop` (once-guarded stop, ticker stopped, lookups bounded by the validated `[dns]` timeout, combined stopper wired into `device.Close`); the liveness-loss trigger is correctly edge-triggered with re-arm on recovery; the controller genuinely runs for a single-hostname peer (`peerNeedsHubFailover` returns true for any `IsName` spec, proven by `TestResolutionSingleHostnameBootAdopts`); ordering is deterministic and suppression is set-membership-based, so record rotation cannot churn the remote.

Two objective, in-scope findings remain.

**1. The "family-filter" clause of the task is not implemented.** The task description requires "on SUCCESS, family-filter/order and call updateResolution", and the seam contract at `/home/pavel/work/safe/fecalia/internal/dnsresolve/resolver.go:11` states "callers filter/order by local path family as needed". `orderAddrPorts` (`/home/pavel/work/safe/fecalia/.claude/worktrees/agent-ac373e4ef09d0a464/internal/device/resolution.go:230`) orders v4-first and dedups, but filters nothing against the local paths' families — the controller does not even receive them. The comment at `resolution.go:145` names "a family the local paths cannot use" as an empty-set cause, yet no code can produce that outcome. Concrete failure mode: v4-only local paths + an AAAA-only answer for the active spec → `updateResolution` sees the v4 `activeAddr` gone, repoints the bond at `addrs[0]` (a v6 address no path socket can send to), and holds it there on every subsequent identical answer — a *successful* lookup tears down a working endpoint set, the exact outcome D46 exists to prevent on the failure path.

**2. The out-of-band re-arm discards the TTL clamp.** `checkLivenessLoss` (`resolution.go:192-198`) ignores `resolveTarget`'s `(minTTL, ttlOk)` and re-arms `nextPollAt` at the full `pollInterval`, contradicting the documented invariant at `resolution.go:123-124` ("a record about to expire is re-checked no later than its TTL") precisely in the hub-loss window where a fresh record matters most. (The re-arm after a *failed* out-of-band resolve is fine — attempt spacing never exceeds `pollInterval`.)
